title: In a planet far far away

content.problemStatement:
CodeChef has sent Jalebi Bai to a new planet to learn a special recipe from them. On her journey from the space station to the head chef's house, she observed that the multiplication rule on this new planet is slightly different from our Earth.

For example: on this planet, 2∗42*42∗4 may be 999 instead of 888. Weird, she thought.

Curious, Jalebi Bai asked a co-passenger the results of all the 100100100 multiplications between all pairs of digits.

She figured out that the Standard algorithm for multiplication on this planet follows the same rules as on Earth except that digit multiplication should follow the rule of that planet.

She also found out that any digit multiplied with 000 is 000, the multiplication of xxx with yyy is the same as the multiplication of yyy with xxx.

Addition is defined to be same as that on the Earth.

For example, if the result of 222 multiplied with 444 is 999, 555 multiplied with 333 is 191919, 222 multiplied with 333 is 171717 and 444 multiplied with 555 is 242424, then multiplication of 252525 with 434343 will give:

`			  25
			  43
		---------------
			  19
			 170
			 240
			 900
		---------------
			1329
		---------------
`The final answer will be 132913291329 (following the addition algorithm of the earth for managing carry).

Finally Jalebi Bai meets the head chef of the planet, but the problem is he is ready to give her the recipe only if she can solve a puzzle. He asks her to find the sum of the results of multiplication (according to the planet) between all possible ordered pairs of numbers from the set of positive integer less than or equal to a given number AAA. **Note:** If iii and jjj are distinct, then the pair (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is considered different from (j,i)(j,i)(j,i).

Jalebi Bai then asks him for a formal multiplication algorithm of the planet, to which the head chef agrees and gives following description.

The multiplication of two numbers XXX and YYY represented as arrays, with MMM as the digit multiplication matrix, is defined as follows:

`Multiply(X[1..p], Y[1..q])
{
    m = [1..p+q]  //Allocate space for result
    for b = 1 to q
    {
        carry = 0
        for a = 1 to p
        {
            m[a + b - 1] += carry + M[X[a]] [Y[b]]
            carry = m[a + b - 1] /10
            m[a + b - 1] = m[a + b - 1] mod 10
        }
        m[b + p] += carry
    }
    answer = 0
    for r = p+q to 1
        answer = answer*10 + m[r]
    return answer
}
`Aliens cannot hear any large number, so they want to hear the output modulo ModModMod, where ModModMod is some integer.

If Jalebi Bai gives a wrong output, she would be eaten up! (Surprise surprise!)

Jalebi Bai can contact someone on earth for help. She reaches you for a code which can evaluate the output for any given input. Can you help her solve this puzzle?


Input Format
The digit multiplication rule appears in 9 lines. Line iii contains the multiplication rule for the digit iii. Each line contains 999 positive integers, each of which is less than 100100100. It is guaranteed that the given matrix is symmetric.


The next line contains TTT, the number of test cases to evaluate.


The first line of each test case contains a positive integer AAA.


The second line of each test case contains ModModMod.



Output Format
For each test case, output the solution modulo ModModMod in a single line.
constraints: 1≤Sum of lengths of all strings A≤5∗1051 \leq \text{Sum of lengths of all strings A} \leq 5*10^51≤Sum of lengths of all strings A≤5∗105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1. Normal multiplication rule follows.
